paragraphs.text: Seventeen poems on industrial grief, inherited silence, and the geography of the eastern French mill towns. Composed between 2017 and 2023 in Mulhouse, Thionville, and the Abbaye de Fontevraud.

They built a hill from what the furnace would not keep,
          a black geometry rising at the edge of the commune,
          and we, children of the late shift, climbed it
          as if it were a saint's relic, knees grey, palms grey.

My uncle said the heap had its own weather —
          a small wind that carried the smell of cooled iron
          and the particular silence of a thing
          that had been useful once and was no longer.

We buried the village inside its own residue.
          We buried, also, the names of the men who knew
          which slag was Tuesday's and which was Thursday's,
          which belonged to the war and which to the peace after.

Now grass goes over it the way forgetting goes over a face,
          patient, indifferent, almost tender.

She came home from the laundry with her fingers already shaped
          for the garden — flat at the tip, calloused at the base,
          a tool the body had agreed to become
          so that the rest of the body could rest.

She planted absences in a row, watered nothing,
          and still, by August, the absences had grown
          into a kind of crop we ate without naming.

I asked her once what she missed of the old country.
          She held up her hands — the trowels — and said:
          I miss the soil that knew them as hands.

And the kitchen, which had been listening,
          drew its curtains an inch tighter against the afternoon.

Fog has come down to the towpath again
          and the barges are merely the rumor of barges,
          long grey shapes that the river refuses to finish
          the sentence of.

A heron stands so still it has joined the architecture.
          A man passes with a dog that does not lift its head.
          The mill on the opposite bank
          has not been a mill since the year my father stopped speaking.

I count the locks downstream — one, two, three —
          the way one counts the rooms of a house
          one is preparing, slowly, to leave.

February has the patience of an undertaker.
          The canal carries the cold without complaint,
          carries it east, toward the border, toward whatever country
          still has use for our weather.

items.text: I.Foundry, 1973— the iron, at last, gave a softer name to morning.
II.Slag Heap Elegy— we buried the village inside its own residue.
III.My Mother's Hands Are Trowels— she planted absences in a row, watered nothing.
IV.The Canal in February— a long grey sentence the river refused to finish.
V.Letter from Hayange— the postman, after that winter, walked only on the shaded side.
VI.Déprise— a word the language keeps in a locked drawer.
VII.Annunciation at the Rolling Mill— and the angel, soot-mouthed, asked nothing of me.
VIII.Inventory of a Father's Toolbox— each handle worn to the shape of someone else's grip.
IX.Thionville, Tuesday— the bells corrected the hour the factory could not keep.
X.Hour That Eats Itself— noon arrived already devoured at the edges.
XI.The Archivist's Refusal— I filed the silence under its proper year.
XII.Slow Train, Metz–Longwy— the windows kept giving back fields we had already crossed.
XIII.Slate Roofs After Rain— even the houses turned their faces toward the dark.
XIV.A Photograph My Grandmother Cropped— the man at the edge had a name we no longer use.
XV.Coke Oven, Sunday— the flame, dismissed, kept its small congregation.
XVI.Wage, Translated— what was paid for, and what was simply taken.
XVII.Threshold, Without Door— I stood there long enough to become the frame.
IV.
The Canal in February

Fog has come down to the towpath again
          and the barges are merely the rumor of barges,
          long grey shapes that the river refuses to finish
          the sentence of.

visibleText: Ashen Meridian Threshold Collection About the Poet Marginalia Correspondence The Hour That Eats Itself Collection Seventeen poems on industrial grief, inherited silence, and the geography of the eastern French mill towns. Composed between 2017 and 2023 in Mulhouse, Thionville, and the Abbaye de Fontevraud. Index of Poems Seventeen, in order. I. Foundry, 1973 — the iron, at last, gave a softer name to morning. II. Slag Heap Elegy — we buried the village inside its own residue. III. My Mother's Hands Are Trowels — she planted absences in a row, watered nothing. IV. The Canal in February — a long grey sentence the river refused to finish. V. Letter from Hayange — the postman, after that winter, walked only on the shaded side. VI. Déprise — a word the language keeps in a locked drawer. VII. Annunciation at the Rolling Mill — and the angel, soot-mouthed, asked nothing of me. VIII. Inventory of a Father's Toolbox — each handle worn to the shape of someone else's grip. IX. Thionville, Tuesday — the bells corrected the hour the factory could not keep. X. Hour That Eats Itself — noon arrived already devoured at the edges. XI. The Archivist's Refusal — I filed the silence under its proper year. XII. Slow Train, Metz–Longwy — the windows kept giving back fields we had already crossed. XIII. Slate Roofs After Rain — even the houses turned their faces toward the dark. XIV. A Photograph My Grandmother Cropped — the man at the edge had a name we no longer use. XV. Coke Oven, Sunday — the flame, dismissed, kept its small congregation. XVI. Wage, Translated — what was paid for, and what was simply taken. XVII. Threshold, Without Door — I stood there long enough to become the frame. Three Poems, In Full Selected from the collection. A language for what the foundries could not say, and what the daughters were left holding. II. Slag Heap Elegy They built a hill from what the furnace would not keep, a black geometry rising at the edge of the commune, and we, children of the late shift, climbed it as if it were a saint's relic, knees grey, palms grey. My uncle said the heap had its own weather — a small wind that carried the smell of cooled iron and the particular silence of a thing that had been useful once and was no longer. We buried the village inside its own residue. We buried, also, the names of the men who knew which slag was Tuesday's and which was Thursday's, which belonged to the war and which to the peace after. Now grass goes over it the way forgetting goes over a face, patient, indifferent, almost tender. III. My Mother's Hands Are Trowels She came home from the laundry with her fingers already shaped for the garden — flat at the tip, calloused at the base, a tool the body had agreed to become so that the rest of the body could rest. She planted absences in a row, watered nothing, and still, by August, the absences had grown into a kind of crop we ate without naming. I asked her once what she missed of the old country. She held up her hands — the trowels — and said: I miss the soil that knew them as hands. And the kitchen, which had been listening, drew its curtains an inch tighter against the afternoon. IV. The Canal in February Fog has come down to the towpath again and the barges are merely the rumor of barges, long grey shapes that the river refuses to finish the sentence of. A heron stands so still it has joined the architecture. A man passes with a dog that does not lift its head. The mill on the opposite bank has not been a mill since the year my father stopped speaking. I count the locks downstream — one, two, three — the way one counts the rooms of a house one is preparing, slowly, to leave. February has the patience of an undertaker. The canal carries the cold without complaint, carries it east, toward the border, toward whatever country still has use for our weather. Ashen Meridian — MMXXIV The Hour That Eats Itself, a debut collection by Solenne Vray. Set in Georgia and system-ui. Printed in a limited edition by Éditions du Seuil Mort, Metz.
